core_intuitions: Chain-rule factorization: modeling a sequence's joint probability is EXACTLY predicting each next token from its prefix, and a causal transformer computes all of these conditionals in a single parallel pass.
The loss IS cross-entropy = negative log-likelihood; minimizing it equals minimizing KL(data || model), and it can never fall below the irreducible entropy floor of the data.
Perplexity = exp(loss) is the effective branching factor: how many equiprobable tokens the model is effectively choosing among at each step.

Teacher forcing + label shift: feed ground-truth tokens, targets are the inputs shifted left by one, so every position is trained in parallel; the causal mask stops each position from peeking at the answer.

Loss masking with packing: concatenate short documents to fill the context window, then zero out padding and cross-document boundary tokens (ignore_index) so empty/contaminated positions never corrupt the gradient; normalize by active-token count.

Tokenizer-agnostic and alternative objectives: bits-per-byte renormalizes per-token loss by bytes/token for fair cross-model comparison, and UL2 shows the objective itself can be a mixture of denoisers (R/X/S).

new_figures.name: ptobj-teacher-forcing-shift
new_figures.concept: Teacher forcing and the shift-by-one alignment: each input position's logit is scored against the NEXT token, and all positions are predicted in parallel from ground-truth inputs.
new_figures.rationale: This is the single most load-bearing mechanic of the chapter and its loss code (inputs = x[:-1], targets = x[1:]). The chapter already links out to the transformer chapter's causal-mask GRID for the mask itself, but nothing here makes the input/target ALIGNMENT and the parallel teacher-forced nature visible. Readers routinely confuse which logit predicts which token; a static alignment diagram fixes that instantly.
new_figures.brief: A clean alignment diagram over one short, concrete illustrative sequence (use [BOS] The cat sat and a trailing <eos>; small integers/positions 0..T only, no benchmark numbers). Lay out two horizontal lanes of equal-width cells, aligned column-by-column. TOP lane, labeled 'INPUT (fed to model): x[0 .. T-1]' = cells [BOS] | The | cat | sat. Between the lanes draw a thin 'model' band that emits, above each input cell, a tiny stacked-bars glyph representing a logit/probability vector over the vocabulary (3-4 little bars, one taller = the predicted token). BOTTOM lane, labeled 'TARGET (input shifted left by one): x[1 .. T]' = cells The | cat | sat | <eos>, each sitting one column to the right conceptually but drawn directly beneath the input column it is predicted FROM. Draw an accent-colored arrow from each input column's logit glyph to the target cell it must predict, with a small caption 'position t predicts token t+1'. Behind the input lane, add faint triangular left-context shading (each cell only reads cells to its left) with a one-line note 'causal mask: position t may read only positions <= t (see the causal-mask grid in the attention chapter)' -- do NOT redraw the full mask grid. Put a headline equation-style callout: 'targets = inputs shifted by one; all T predictions computed in ONE parallel forward pass'. At the very bottom, a compact two-state contrast strip: left box 'Training (teacher forcing): parallel, ground-truth tokens as input'; right box 'Inference: sequential, model feeds its OWN previous output back'. Theme-safe: use var(--...) with fallbacks and the v-accent / v-muted / v-fill / v-grid classes; accent only for the predict-arrows and the tall predicted bar. Everything must fit the viewBox with no overlapping labels; clear left-to-right, top-to-bottom reading order.

new_figures.name: ptobj-crossentropy-entropy-floor
new_figures.concept: Cross-entropy decomposes into an irreducible data-entropy floor plus a KL gap that training shrinks; perplexity = exp(loss) reads that same loss as an effective branching factor.
new_figures.rationale: The information-theoretic viewpoint (H(p,q) = H(p) + KL(p||q), the entropy floor no model can beat, and perplexity as branching factor) is the most abstract and most misunderstood idea in the chapter. A single static infographic that ties the decomposition, the un-beatable floor, and the loss->perplexity mapping together builds the intuition that a stalling loss may just be near the floor, and that lower loss = fewer effective choices.
new_figures.brief: Left two-thirds: a vertical 'loss bar' measured in nats/token on a labeled y-axis. The full bar height = the model's cross-entropy H(p_data, p_theta). Split it into two stacked segments: the BOTTOM segment (use v-fill / muted) labeled 'H(p_data): irreducible entropy floor -- the loss no model can beat', capped by a bold dashed horizontal 'floor' line spanning the panel; the TOP segment (use v-accent) labeled 'D_KL(p_data || p_theta): the gap training shrinks'. A downward arrow running alongside the bar reads 'training pushes the model down, closing the KL gap toward the floor -- it can never cross below it'. Show the decomposition as a caption/equation: H(p,q) = H(p) + D_KL(p || q). Right third: the loss->perplexity mapping. Show PPL = exp(loss) as a small label, then draw two 'branching fans' -- a single node splitting into several equiprobable next-token branches -- one NARROW fan (few branches) tied by a connector to a LOWER point on the loss axis and labeled 'low loss = confident, few effective choices', one WIDE fan (more branches) tied to a HIGHER point and labeled 'high loss = uncertain, many effective choices'. Caption the fans 'perplexity = effective branching factor (as uncertain as a uniform choice among PPL tokens)'. Use only small illustrative branch counts and mark them 'illustrative' -- invent NO perplexity/entropy benchmark numbers; the y-axis carries generic tick labels, not measured values. Theme-safe via var(--...) fallbacks and v-* classes; accent reserved for the KL segment and the wider fan. All labels legible, no overlaps, fits viewBox.

new_figures.name: ptobj-packing-loss-mask
new_figures.concept: Document packing fills the context window instead of wasting it on padding; a loss mask then zeros out padding AND cross-document boundary tokens to prevent contamination.
new_figures.rationale: Packing and loss masking are two adjacent sections that only make sense together, and both are inherently spatial (a fixed-width window of cells). A static before/after strip with an aligned mask track makes the efficiency win AND the exact positions that must be excluded (padding + the first token of each new doc) concrete in one glance -- far clearer than the ASCII sketches currently in the text.
new_figures.brief: Represent a fixed context window of L cells (use a small illustrative L, e.g. ~14-16 cells; small integers only, no benchmark waste percentages as data). THREE aligned horizontal tracks of the same width, stacked. TRACK 1 'Unpacked (padded)': a short Doc A of a few filled cells at the left, then a long tail of hatched/greyed 'PAD' cells; bracket the PAD tail with a qualitative label 'wasted compute -- no gradient signal'. TRACK 2 'Packed': Doc A cells, a distinct 'SEP' cell, Doc B cells, 'SEP', Doc C cells, then a short 'PAD' tail. Color-code the three docs with theme-safe tints (use opacity variants of a single accent, or v-fill vs v-accent vs v-grid fills -- do NOT hardcode primary colors) and label each block Doc A / Doc B / Doc C. TRACK 3 'Loss mask (train on this position?)': one cell per column beneath the packed track, marked check (train) for interior tokens and cross (masked) at every PAD cell AND at the FIRST token of Doc B and the FIRST token of Doc C. Draw a short accent arrow from Doc A's last token to Doc B's first token labeled 'prediction poisoned by unrelated prior document -> masked'. Caption strip: 'Packing recovers the wasted tokens; padding and cross-document boundary positions are excluded from the loss (ignore_index = -100), and the loss is normalized by active-token count.' Keep the 'wasted' vs 'recovered' framing qualitative. Theme-safe var(--...) fallbacks and v-* classes; legible cell labels, no overlaps, entire figure inside viewBox with a clear top-to-bottom reading order.

new_figures.name: ptobj-ul2-denoising-modes
new_figures.concept: The three UL2 denoising modes (R / X / S) shown on one shared sentence: how much is corrupted, in what shape, and what the model must predict.
new_figures.rationale: The UL2 section is dense and entirely table/text based. Because R/X/S differ only in corruption AMOUNT and SHAPE, they are far easier to grasp side-by-side on a single sentence than in prose. This is a nice-to-have that turns an alternative-objectives section into an at-a-glance comparison, but it is secondary to the causal-LM core, hence lower priority.
new_figures.brief: The SAME short source sentence rendered as a token strip three times, stacked, each row labeled with its mode. ROW 1 'R-denoising (Regular): short spans, ~15% corrupted -> recall': a couple of short contiguous spans replaced by sentinel glyphs [X0], [X1]; to the right, the TARGET strip = the removed spans keyed by sentinel. ROW 2 'X-denoising (Extreme): long spans, ~50-70% corrupted -> generation': most of the sentence replaced by sentinels, only a few anchor tokens kept; target strip = the long removed spans. ROW 3 'S-denoising (Sequential / prefix causal): predict the suffix from the prefix': a clean kept PREFIX on the left up to a split marker, then the entire SUFFIX shown as the to-be-predicted continuation; target strip = the suffix. Include a compact legend: kept token, masked span, sentinel token [Xk], predicted target. Caption: 'One model (UL2) is pretrained on a MIXTURE of these modes; at inference a mode sentinel selects the behavior.' The 15% and 50-70% figures are definitional to UL2 (echo from the chapter's table, not invented benchmarks) -- render them as descriptive labels only. Theme-safe var(--...) fallbacks and v-* classes; sentinels and masked spans use accent/fill tints, kept tokens neutral; keep every token cell and label legible with no overlap and the whole thing inside the viewBox.

summary: This chapter has ZERO figures yet is one of the most concept-dense in the book, so it is genuinely figure-poor and warrants a small foundational set rather than mere decoration. I propose three priority-1/2 static infographics for its core mechanics and one priority-3 nice-to-have. (1) ptobj-teacher-forcing-shift makes the input/target shift-by-one alignment and the parallel teacher-forced pass visible -- the single most load-bearing idea behind the loss code -- deliberately NOT redrawing the causal-mask grid the chapter already links to in the attention chapter. (2) ptobj-crossentropy-entropy-floor ties together the abstract information-theoretic core: CE = entropy floor + KL gap, the un-beatable floor, and perplexity = exp(loss) as an effective branching factor. (3) ptobj-packing-loss-mask renders packing + loss masking together as a fixed-width window with an aligned mask track, showing exactly which padding and cross-document boundary positions get excluded. (4, priority 3) ptobj-ul2-denoising-modes turns the text-only UL2 section into an at-a-glance R/X/S side-by-side. All are static -- no core intuition here genuinely requires motion, so I kept the set disciplined and single-frame-legible. Everything is theme-safe (var(--...) fallbacks + v-* classes, no hardcoded primaries), uses only small illustrative integers, and invents no benchmark numbers.
